figures.gloss: Dog-headed twin of Quetzalcoatl who guides the sun, and the dead, through the underworld.
figures.facet: The dog who walks the dead home — which is why a dog was buried with the Aztec deceased.

figures.gloss: The dual 'Two-God', lord-and-lady of the highest heaven, from whom the four Tezcatlipocas are born.
figures.facet: Not a father but a father-mother in one — the highest sovereignty imagined as an irreducible pair.

figures.gloss: Black (North), Red/Xipe Totec (East), Blue/Huitzilopochtli (South), White/Quetzalcoatl (West) — the sons of the dual god who quarter the cosmos and hold its balance.
figures.facet: Sovereignty as four rival brothers in dynamic tension — order as a standoff, not a throne.

figures.gloss: The Aztec board game whose cross-shaped board is a quincunx — four arms aligned to the four cardinal directions around a center, a 52-space circuit matching the Calendar Round. Played for ruinous stakes under Macuilxōchitl, god of games, and consulted as an oracle.
figures.facet: The board IS the quartered cosmos: to play is to walk a token through the four directions of the world — and to read fate by doing so. The four-directions worldview made playable.
figures.provenance: Board-as-four-directions cosmogram is attested — Caso found the stone Pedregal board aligned exactly to the cardinal points; the 52-space circuit = the Calendar Round. Oracle/divination use is well-founded scholarship (Durán; Mexicolore). The modern 'four players each embody a direction' overlay is NOT pre-Columbian and is deliberately left out.

figures.gloss: The militant solar deity and tribal patron of the Mexica, born fully armed from Coatlicue to slay his treasonous sister. He demands continuous nourishment via human blood and hearts to sustain the sun's journey and stave off cosmic collapse.
figures.facet: He is a radically hyper-localized patron whose cosmic maintenance is entirely contingent on the political expansion and mechanized ritual slaughter of the Aztec state. He turns imperialism into a cosmological necessity.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagun, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); Diego Duran, 'The History of the Indies of New Spain' (1581).

figures.gloss: The omnipotent, invisible, and capricious lord of the night sky, destiny, and sorcery. He observes all human deeds through his obsidian mirror, raising slaves to royalty and casting kings into ruin purely at his whim.
figures.facet: Unlike standard cosmic adversaries, he is neither evil nor opposed to the highest order; he *is* the arbitrary nature of fate itself, enforcing absolute humility through the unpredictable reversal of human fortune. His attribute is an obsidian mirror that shows the soul its own hidden face — this whole mirror is his instrument.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagun, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); 'Codex Borgia' (pre-Columbian).

figures.gloss: The terrifying earth goddess who gives birth to the sun, moon, and stars. Depicted decapitated, her blood forming twin serpents, she wears a skirt of writhing snakes and a necklace of human hearts, hands, and skulls.
figures.facet: She refuses the comforting tropes of maternal warmth, embodying the earth as a devouring maw that both continuously births the cosmos and relentlessly consumes the dead in a closed loop of violent vital energy.
figures.provenance: 'Florentine Codex' (1577); The Coatlicue monolith (discovered 1790).

figures.gloss: The ancient, goggle-eyed god of rain, lightning, and agricultural fertility. He rules Tlalocan, a verdant watery afterlife for those who die by drowning or lightning strikes, but demands the tears of sacrificed children to release the rains.
figures.facet: He enforces a ruthless transactional ecology: human tears are literally converted into rain. The terror of his child sacrifices reflects the raw, existential dread of drought in the Valley of Mexico.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagun, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); 'Codex Borgia'.

figures.gloss: The benevolent creator deity who descended to the underworld to retrieve the bones of past generations, bleeding upon them to create modern humanity. He brought maize, the calendar, and the arts to mankind before departing into the east.
figures.facet: He represents the dual tension of Mesoamerican life: the sky (feather) and the earth (serpent). He uniquely bleeds *himself* for humanity, contrasting with the deities who primarily demand humans bleed for them.
figures.provenance: 'Annals of Cuauhtitlan' (1570); 'Florentine Codex' (1577).

figures.gloss: The current, fierce sun of the Fifth Age, born from the self-immolation of the crippled god Nanahuatzin. He is depicted at the center of the Aztec calendar stone, his tongue shaped as an obsidian sacrificial knife demanding blood.
figures.facet: He is a fundamentally fragile celestial body. Because he was born from sacrifice, he requires perpetual, agonizing reciprocity to simply move across the sky, anchoring the Aztec anxiety of impending apocalypse.
figures.provenance: 'Leyenda de los Soles' (1558); The Aztec Sun Stone (discovered 1790).

figures.gloss: A human captive or slave who is bathed, dressed, and ritually transformed into the living embodiment of a god. For a set period, they are treated with utter reverence before being sacrificed to reenact the deity's mythic death.
figures.facet: It breaks the boundary between offering and receiver; the sacrifice *becomes* the god dying to renew the god. The flayed human skin worn by priests afterward makes this ontological merger viscerally literal. The Aztec word for this mirror's own thesis: one force, countless eyes — the god plays through the human the way the player plays the game.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagun, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); Inga Clendinnen, 'Aztecs: An Interpretation' (1991).

figures.gloss: The singular, dynamic, and pantheistic sacred energy that permeates all existence. Rather than being a static creator god, it is an ever-flowing, dualistic process of becoming that constantly generates, destroys, and regenerates the cosmos.
figures.facet: It eliminates the Western distinction between the sacred and the profane, or creator and creation. Deities are merely temporary, concentrated nodes or 'masks' of this single unfolding cosmic electricity.
figures.provenance: James Maffie, 'Aztec Philosophy: Understanding a World in Motion' (2014); 'Florentine Codex' (1577).

figures.gloss: The skeletal ruler of Mictlan, the lowest level of the underworld where most souls journey after death. He sits with his wife Mictecacihuatl in a windowless house, receiving the dead regardless of their moral standing in life.
figures.facet: He strips morality from the afterlife. The journey to his realm is a physically grueling dissolution of the self, culminating in the soul's complete annihilation rather than eternal reward or punishment.
figures.provenance: 'Codex Borgia' (pre-Columbian); 'Florentine Codex' (1577).

figures.gloss: God of vegetative renewal, springtime, warfare and goldsmiths, one of the four sons of Ometeotl. During the festival of Tlacaxipehualiztli his priests wore the flayed skins of sacrificed captives for twenty days, the rotting skin worn as the husk from which new growth pushes.
figures.facet: The shed human skin is made a literal agricultural metaphor: the old husk must rot on a living body before the new seed-skin beneath can show, renewal staged as endurance rather than instant transformation.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagún, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); Diego Durán, 'The Book of the Gods and Rites' (1574-76).

figures.gloss: Goddess of lakes, rivers, springs and the newborn, and consort of Tlaloc. She presided over the baptismal bathing rite performed for Aztec infants and, in the Legend of the Five Suns, ruled the Fourth Sun, an age that ended when she wept the world into flood.
figures.facet: She governs water at its two most intimate points of contact with a human life — the bath that opens it and the flood that can end it — sovereignty of the same element at birth and at apocalypse.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagún, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); 'Leyenda de los Soles' (1558).

figures.gloss: Moon goddess and eldest daughter of Coatlicue, who led her four-hundred siblings, the Centzon Huitznahua, to kill their mother upon learning of her miraculous pregnancy. Huitzilopochtli sprang from the womb fully armed, beheaded her, and flung her dismembered body down the sacred mountain Coatepec.
figures.facet: Her carved, dismembered relief was set at the very foot of the Templo Mayor's Huitzilopochtli stairway, so that every human sacrifice thrown down those steps reenacted her fall — one myth engineered into the state's central architecture.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagún, 'Florentine Codex', Book 3 (1577); the Coyolxauhqui monolith (discovered 1978, Templo Mayor).

figures.gloss: Stellar god of the hunt, the fire-drill and the Milky Way, worshipped as the deified ancestor of the nomadic Chichimec peoples. By the noblewoman Chimalman he fathered Quetzalcoatl, and his cult centered on the Quecholli festival of the hunt.
figures.facet: He grounds the settled, agricultural Mexica in a remembered northern origin as hunting nomads — the star-god through whom an urban empire keeps a Chichimec pedigree alive.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagún, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); 'Historia de los mexicanos por sus pinturas' (c. 1530).

figures.gloss: Earth-and-lust goddess of sexual transgression and purification, who both provokes moral 'filth' and, in a single deathbed confession, devours it whole. Depicted with a black mouth and holding a broom, she is closely tied to the moon, cotton-spinning and midwifery.
figures.facet: Sin and its remedy are placed in the same hand: the goddess who tempts is also the only power who can absolve, and absolution is available exactly once, at the very end of life.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagún, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); 'Codex Borbonicus' (pre-Columbian/early colonial).

figures.gloss: Young goddess of love, flowers, weaving, and pleasure, and patron of artisans, pregnant women and prostitutes. She was abducted into the underworld by Tezcatlipoca in one myth cycle and is closely tied to Xochipilli as complementary powers of feast and beauty.
figures.facet: She is set against the earthy Tlazolteotl as the two poles of the feminine in Aztec thought — pleasure kept young, ornamental and desirable versus pleasure as the transgression that must later be confessed away.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagún, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); 'Codex Borgia'.

figures.gloss: Young maize deity, in most accounts the child of Tlazolteotl, whose growth cycle from planted seed to harvested ear underlies much of the agricultural festival calendar, notably the Ochpaniztli rites of Toci and the harvest.
figures.facet: The staple crop is given a face and a birth so that an entire year's planting and reaping can be narrated as a single divine life cycle rather than mere subsistence labor.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagún, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); 'Historia de los mexicanos por sus pinturas' (c. 1530).

figures.gloss: Fire-lord of the cosmic center and the domestic hearth, among the oldest deities in the Mesoamerican pantheon. He was honored above all in the Toxiuhmolpilia, the New Fire ceremony held every 52 years atop the Hill of the Star to avert the world's end.
figures.facet: Every household's hearth-fire and the entire calendar's 52-year wheel answer to the same god — the smallest domestic flame and the largest unit of cosmic time governed as one continuous fire.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagún, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); 'Codex Borbonicus'.

figures.gloss: Skeletal queen of Mictlan and co-ruler with her husband Mictlantecuhtli, keeping the bones of past generations in the lowest, windowless level of the underworld and presiding over the Miccailhuitontli and Hueymiccaihuitl feasts of the dead.
figures.facet: Rulership of the dead is never solitary in this cosmology — the underworld is run as a married household, morally neutral toward every soul that arrives regardless of how it lived.
figures.provenance: 'Codex Borgia' (pre-Columbian); Bernardino de Sahagún, 'Florentine Codex' (1577).

figures.gloss: Fierce earth-and-birth goddess of Culhuacan, patroness of the Cihuateteo, the deified spirits of women who died in childbirth. Her night-wandering cry, wailing for lost children, was recorded as an omen of the coming Spanish conquest, and her title also named the Aztec state's chief minister and war-co-ruler.
figures.facet: A single name spans both a terrifying goddess of the birthing-bed and the second-highest office of Aztec government — motherhood and statecraft fused into one title of fierce, necessary authority.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagún, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); Diego Durán, 'The History of the Indies of New Spain' (1581).

figures.gloss: God of art, song, dance, games and entheogenic plants, patron of feasting and of the ballgame's leisured pleasures, the male counterpart to Xochiquetzal. His famous Tlalmanalco statue is carved with stylized psilocybin mushrooms, morning glory and tobacco flowers across its base.
figures.facet: Pleasure itself is elevated to a full deity-portfolio rather than a byproduct of worship — feasting, music and intoxication given the same institutional seriousness as war or agriculture.
figures.provenance: Bernardino de Sahagún, 'Florentine Codex' (1577); the Xochipilli statue (Museo Nacional de Antropología).

figures.gloss: A humble, disease-covered god who, at the divine council held in darkness at Teotihuacan, was the only one to unhesitatingly leap into the sacrificial bonfire when the wealthier, more beautiful Tecciztecatl faltered. His self-immolation is the act by which the Fifth Sun, Tonatiuh, is born.
figures.facet: Cosmic legitimacy is awarded to poverty and courage rather than status and beauty — the current sun exists because the least among the gods was willing to burn first.
figures.provenance: 'Leyenda de los Soles' (1558); 'Historia de los mexicanos por sus pinturas' (c. 1530).

figures.gloss: A monstrous, caiman-or-toad-like earth being, alive at the beginning of the current age, whom Tezcatlipoca and Quetzalcoatl seized and tore in two to make the earth from one half and the sky from the other. Her body's hills, valleys and springs are her own scarred anatomy, and she was said to cry out at night, demanding hearts and blood to be consoled.
figures.facet: Unlike Coatlicue's ongoing cycle of birth-and-devouring, Tlaltecuhtli is a one-time act of violent world-carpentry — the ground itself is a dismembered body that never stopped hurting.
figures.provenance: 'Historia de los mexicanos por sus pinturas' (c. 1530); 'Histoyre du Mechique' (1547); the Tlaltecuhtli monolith (discovered 2006, Templo Mayor).

figures.gloss: The Mexica cosmological history of four prior world-ages, each ruled by a different god and each destroyed in turn — by jaguars, by hurricane winds, by fiery rain, and by flood — followed by the present, Fifth Sun, born from Nanahuatzin's sacrifice and destined to end in earthquake.
figures.facet: Catastrophe is not an exception to this cosmos but its operating principle: the current world is explicitly the fifth attempt, its predecessors' failures the reason present-day ritual maintenance is treated as urgent rather than optional.
figures.provenance: 'Leyenda de los Soles' (1558); 'Historia de los mexicanos por sus pinturas' (c. 1530); Codex Chimalpopoca.

edges.note: The sun whose daily survival must be paid for — by slaying (Ra) or by feeding (Huitzilopochtli).
A single sacred reality of which all gods and things are passing faces.
The unconditioned ground from which a knowable, named divinity unfolds — a far reach across very different grammars.
The supreme principle as an irreducible male-female pair: Heaven/Earth, Lord/Lady.
The mechanism of the One Through Many: teōtl acts in the world by 'wearing' an ixiptla.
The board's four arms are the four directional quarters the Tezcatlipocas govern — the cosmogram the game is played on.
Quincunx: four directions around a green center — the board and the world-tree are the same diagram.
Four quarters around a green center — the directional gods and the world-tree are one diagram.

A reach: both are great serpent creator-beings central to their worlds, but Quetzalcoatl is a feathered culture-hero god of wind and learning while the Rainbow Serpent is the water-snake of the land's own making. Serpent-as-cosmos in both, but the resemblance is loose. Flagged.

Both name a single sacred ground-power immanent in all things rather than a god above them — the Dreaming and the Mesoamerican teotl — the divine as the very fabric of what-is, expressed through countless particular beings. One-through-many reached by unrelated peoples.

Two rain-and-storm powers with great staring eyes whose images must be served to keep the rains coming — the haloed mouthless Wandjina repainted in the rock, and the goggle-eyed rain-god fed in his mountains. The rain made a face that maintenance keeps alive, by unrelated peoples.

Loose: both fuse the sacred being with its physical embodiment so that tending the image IS tending the god — the Wandjina that became its own painting, the Mesoamerican ixiptla that the deity inhabits. Presence-in-the-image in both, but the mechanisms differ sharply. Flagged.
Old-woman Earth who both bears and devours, mother of crops and grave of the dead.
A reach: both name the many sacred powers as faces of a single diffused divinity — the one-through-many. Speculative, no contact.
GOLD SEAM: Dark, terrifying forces that govern the necessary descent into the underworld to strip away the old form before new life can emerge.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The monstrous, fanged chthonic creator whose absolute fertility is entirely dependent on continuous decapitation and human blood.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Mountain-dwelling storm and rain gods of the Americas whose life-giving waters require intense, sometimes bloody, propitiation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Lords of the pre-Columbian American underworlds, governing the deep earth and the realm of the dead.
Intra-tradition: Co-creators and rival martial patrons who dominate the Aztec imperial cult.
Intra-tradition: The great cosmic rivalry, balancing the civilizing creation of Quetzalcoatl against the destructive caprice of Tezcatlipoca.
Intra-tradition: The tribal war god who is ritually tasked with continuously feeding and sustaining the fragile Fifth Sun.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Militant, dynamic patron deities associated with fire, storms, and royal authority.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Warrior deities who slay a primordial serpent or sibling right after birth to establish cosmic dominance and order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shape-shifting, capricious agents of fate who undermine the established order without being defined by purely moral evil.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Terrifying, martial mother-goddesses demanding blood offerings and adorned with severed body parts.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fundamental earth mother who births both the celestial gods and the monstrous forces that oppose them.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ancient storm and rain gods whose vital agricultural fertility is secured through the terrifying ritual sacrifice of children.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Culture heroes who bypass or defy the other gods to bleed for humanity and bring them civilization.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Civilizing deities who undergo a journey to the underworld and become deeply associated with cyclical resurrection and agriculture.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Solar deities whose daily journey across the sky requires the continuous defeat of cosmic darkness, sustained by human ritual.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Impassive, skeletal lords of the dead who rule over a morally neutral, dreary underworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Subterranean sovereigns commanding the cold realms where human souls eventually descend and dissolve.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An impersonal, flowing principle of cosmic dualism and constant generation rather than a static creator god.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The proxy figure who must be ritually slain to ensure agricultural and cosmic renewal.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A volatile war deity whose martial power is intrinsically linked to the sky and the defense of the state.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A living human who serves as the flawless, structural embodiment of divine and cosmic order for the state.

GOLD SEAM: A speculative but striking structural parallel: a human vessel who becomes the flawless physical manifestation of the divine, though utterly detached from the mesoamerican sacrificial context.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmos fundamentally defined not by a singular moral good, but by an inherently dualistic, self-balancing engine of creation and destruction.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Terrifying, fanged, monstrous mother figures draped in the iconography of death, yet fundamentally venerated as necessary cosmic forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dynamic, impersonal cosmic energy that underlies all existence and demands heavy ritual management to prevent disaster.
The serpent as benign cosmic principle rather than monster — feathered or fiery, consort to creation rather than its enemy.
GOLD SEAM: Earth goddesses strongly associated with caves and the subterranean realm, serving as both maternal protectors and primordial forces.

Speculative: the splitting of the primordial source into a luminous and a dark lineage at the origin loosely rhymes with the dual-natured Ometeotl from whom the paired ordering powers issue — a reach of the dualising-origin theme, flagged.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve the radical identification of a human practitioner with a divine being, though Vajrayana operates via mental visualization while Mesoamerican Ixiptla relied on physical ritual embodiment.
Sovereign of the land of the dead through which all the unliberated must pass — a structural parallel of the underworld-king, no contact.
One sacred reality refracted into countless masks (ixiptla / nirmāṇakāya) — the one-through-many reached independently in Mesoamerica and in Mahāyāna.
Time as successive worlds destroyed and remade — the Mesoamerican Five Suns and the Buddhist kalpas both read history as recurring catastrophe-and-renewal, independently.
Speculative: the lord of delusion and the smoking-mirror lord of fate and temptation both rule the realm of binding desire — a reach of theme, no descent; flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A physical receptacle—whether wood, fur, or human—that serves as the literal, localized housing for a divine force requiring biological sustenance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are solar entities conceptualized as fierce powers that require human lives to sustain their fiery cosmic trajectory.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both elevate gambling from a human pastime to a cosmic mechanism, where the fate of the world or the individual is decided by the dice or bones.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve the mythic narrative of a culturally foundational figure expected to return from over the sea.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme cosmic force that is not a localized deity but the pervasive, immanent energy moving through all things.
GOLD SEAM: Spanish colonizers intentionally conflated the Mesoamerican lord of the dead with the Christian Devil, projecting moral evil onto a morally neutral cosmic function.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Viewing the universe not as a container for deities, but as being composed entirely of a unified, self-generating sacred energy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The terrifying, primordial earth mother whose profound vitality is inextricably linked to visceral destruction and blood offering.
A single bisexual godhead held as a male-female unity — the Ngaju two-in-one parallels the Nahua Lord/Lady of Duality.

A loose parallel — both are great serpent-beings of cosmic standing, but Quetzalcoatl is sky-feathered culture-bringer where Tambon is the underworld-water body; resemblance is the serpent-emblem, not the role.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The visceral embodiment of the rain and storm, bringing both life-sustaining water and the lethal threat of lightning.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The shared belief that those killed by lightning bypass normal death and are instead instantly chosen for a sacred spiritual existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ritual tearing of living flesh to intimately consume the divine essence, bypassing symbolic abstraction for terrifying, physical communion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The intersection of games of chance and cosmic destiny, where throwing dice or drawing lots acts as a microcosm of the gods' gambling with human fate.
Both render the first divine reality as androgynous and paired before differentiation; a reach across families, flagged — twinned primordial wholeness.
Both pair a disordering trickster-power against an ordering creator-sibling; offered cautiously, the adversary as the necessary counterweight to order.
Both encode the whole cosmos as a single geometric figure to be read — the world-egg's spiral beside the quartered board; a flagged cosmogram-rhyme, no contact.

Two young war-gods born to fierce mothers, each a solar warrior who dances victory and is honoured on heights — Murugan of the Tamil hills and Huitzilopochtli of the Mexica, the youthful martial sun reached independently.

An impersonal sacred potency saturating the world beneath the named gods — aṉaṅku as charged dangerous power and teōtl as the single sacred force refracted into deities; the holy-as-energy reached independently in Tamil country and in Mexico.
GOLD SEAM: Benevolent, traveling culture-heroes who explicitly gift the staple crop (wheat/maize) to humanity to ensure their civilization and survival.
GOLD SEAM: Animal-associated chthonic guides or figures structurally tethered to a catastrophic descent into the underworld.
Independent underworld-sovereigns of unrelated traditions — both grim enthroned lords of a chthonic realm, convergence on the death-king joint.
Two quartered-cosmos grids of unrelated peoples: the templum's sixteen sky-houses on the liver and the four-directions board of Patolli, the cosmos drawn as a divided field to be read or played.
The quartered heaven as a working diagram — sixteen regiones around the rim echoing the four-cornered cosmos held by the four Tezcatlipocas; the axis-and-quarters joint surfacing on two continents.
Comparable conceptions of an all-pervading sacred order made legible through ritual specialists — the Etruscan revealed-law beside the Nahua teotl as ground of the read cosmos.
Both are teacher-heroes who deliver the sacred calendar and ritual knowledge to a people, then withdraw — Tages sinks into the furrow, Quetzalcoatl departs over the sea.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme creator entity that is fundamentally a male-female dyad in perfect equilibrium.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fearsome, monstrous escort and adversary encountered on the perilous physical journey through the underworld.

Both render the first division of the cosmos from an undifferentiated whole; the Finnish egg-halves and the Nahua dual-source are far-flung, unrelated answers to the same question — a distant rhyme, flagged.
Both reduce destiny to a casting-system over a fixed sign-set; offered cautiously, no contact — divination and board-cosmogram as two readings of fate.
Both name a single dual creator of paired male/female aspect at the head of the cosmos — divinity as a self-complementary couple.
The serpent as cosmic sustainer and sky-bridge rather than slain dragon — feathered/rainbow snake as continuity, not chaos.
The earth-mother who gives life and deals death in one body — terrible fertility shared across continents.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both figures embody the dual, terrifyingly fertile nature of the earth, holding the inseparable powers of both creation and destruction within the feminine.
GOLD SEAM: Systems originally bearing elements of chance that function as rigorous maps of cosmic destiny, calendrical cycles, and divine forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both intimately link the imagery of monstrous or shadowy dogs with the dangerous, chthonic forces of the underworld.
GOLD SEAM: Fearsome, shifting powers intrinsically tied to the apex feline, embodying the lethal, unpredictable wilderness at the edge of civilization.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are dark, demanding, omnipotent forces requiring rigorous, often severe propitiation to avert their devastating cosmic whims.

Both the obsidian-or-flint-bodied disruptive power set against a brighter creator-brother — Tawiskaron against Sapling, dark Tezcatlipoca against Quetzalcoatl; a loose stone-mirror/flint adversary rhyme across the Americas, flagged as a reach.
Orenda and teōtl both name a pervasive sacred energy rather than a personal high god — the impersonal holy power-stuff of the cosmos, arrived at on opposite ends of the continent without contact.

The great-mother who both brings forth and devours — Pele who builds the land in the same eruption that buries it, Coatlicue the earth-mother of birth and the maw; the creative-destructive feminine ground reached on either side of the Pacific.

Mana and Nahua teōtl both name a pervasive sacred energy filling the cosmos rather than a single high god — the impersonal holy power-stuff that manifests in differing concentrations; the same sacred-power intuition on opposite shores of the Pacific.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A recreational game format structurally transformed into a profound cosmological and divinatory map of the universe's mechanics.

A reach across families: both are deposed-yet-scheming powers driving the cosmos through cycles of overthrow — but no contact whatsoever; flagged speculative, a bare structural rhyme of the eternal usurper.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The sovereign of rain and subterranean waters whose domain demands deep respect and who can bestow sudden wealth or storms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Neither is a mere symbol; both the Mojo and the Ixiptla are constructed physical vessels transubstantiated to literally house and radiate a living spiritual force.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are nocturnal, morally ambiguous lords of the crossroads who test wanderers in the dark, granting tremendous worldly power to those who show no fear.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are monumental, highly adorned earth mothers who hold the duality of life-giving fertility and the consuming grave.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are terrifying, animalistic guardians who oversee the dead and protect them from the spiritual hazards of the underworld.

Both are an earth-mother who is womb and tomb at once, bearing crops and devouring the dead — but Coatlicue is a fearsome serpent-skirted devourer where Ala is the gentle moral ground. A loose structural echo, flagged.
Both name a single all-pervading sacred power/process behind the many spirits rather than a personal high god — order as substance, not sovereign.
Both are the founder-king of the arts who finally departs the kingdom he taught — agriculture, writing and law given, then the throne abandoned for a higher liberation.

Both are feminine divine figures associated with the earth and the underworld — but the Shekhinah is a presence of light and holiness, while Coatlicue is a fearsome earth-mother of life and death. A loose great-mother echo, flagged.
Both are psychopomps who guide the dead — but Xolotl is a dog-headed god of the underworld, while Elijah is a human prophet who never died. A loose structural echo of the psychopomp joint, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The grotesque, dog-like or clownish twin of the high god who descends to aid humanity and navigate the lower realms.

The fourfold quartered cosmos — four directional powers / four moments turning around a still centre — appears in both Kongo and Mesoamerica with no possible contact. A striking structural convergence.
Both are quartered-cosmos diagrams: a cross-in-a-circle whose four arms map the directions and the turning of time. The cosmogram and the gameboard as the same fourfold glyph.
Both govern an underworld reached by crossing waters, but Kalunga is a threshold-line and inversion rather than an enthroned lord of the dead — a loose echo, flagged.
A reach: both diffuse one sacred power into the many faces of the landscape — the one-through-many. Speculative, no contact.
Both make a fashioned object/body into a true vessel of sacred presence — the nkisi inhabited by a captured dead, the ixiptla by a god. A reach across unrelated rites, flagged.
Both name a primal dual-gendered unity prior to division, but the parallel is interpretive and Mahungu is a first human rather than a creator-pair. Flagged as a reach.
A descending divine benefactor who brings grain, law and the civilised arts to a people — analogous culture-hero/civiliser across wholly separate worlds.

Two American conceptions of a single sacred power-stuff diffused through all things — wakȟáŋ and teōtl alike a holy energy pervading the cosmos rather than a personal high god; the panentheist intuition reached twice in the Americas.

Both stand behind the working powers as the source-totality, but the parallel is loose — Ometeotl a self-existent dual deity, Wakáŋ Tȟáŋka a mystery and relatedness, not a maker; a faint one-through-many echo only.
Both the restless, deceiving intelligence loose within the sacred order, but the weight differs — Tezcatlipoca a dread omnipotent power, Iktómi a comic primal namer; a loose trickster rhyme, flagged.

American culture-bringers who deliver the sacred arts and the rites of right living and then depart in transfigured form — the Feathered Serpent and the White Buffalo Calf Woman, revelation that walks in and walks out.

Two New-World solar cults whose great rite feeds the sun with offered flesh — but inverted: Huitzilopochtli is fed the captive's heart, Wí is repaid the dancer's OWN flesh; sacrifice of the other against sacrifice of the self.
Lords of the waters and the deep whose bones and storms govern flood and rain; the Lakota water-monster and the Mesoamerican rain-lord both rule the perilous gift of water from below and above.

The four-directions cosmogram made the ground-plan of a sacred figure — the Lakota hoop-and-cross and the Mesoamerican quartered board both lay the quartered cosmos flat as a usable diagram of the world.

The world held by its four quarters, each a coloured power with its own quarter of the sky — the four winds of the Lakota hoop answering the four Tezcatlipocas of the four directions, the quartered cosmos in two Americas.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The living human being who becomes the literal, moving embodiment and representative of the deity within the community.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A chaotic, deeply terrestrial, and destructive sovereign whose era must end to pave the way for a more structured, orderly epoch.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are symbolically linked to the morning star (Venus) and serve as cultural heroes who brought knowledge to mankind.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are gods who govern the chaotic, raw aggression and initiation rites of youthful warrior bands.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A singular supreme deity composed of a dualistic, perfectly balanced nature of opposing forces.

Sovereignty figured as a fourfold unity: Ngenechen invoked as old-man/old-woman/young-man/young-woman rhymes with the single Tezcatlipoca quartered into four directional sons — the one ruler that can only be named as four, reached independently in two American cosmoses.

The supreme as an internally-dual/complementary principle — Ngenechen's male-female, elder-youthful unity beside the Nahua 'Lord-and-Lady of Duality'; the high god as built-in complementarity rather than a lone king, two American witnesses to the same shape.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The direct Maya expression of the pan-Mesoamerican feathered serpent creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The direct Maya equivalent of the Central Mexican storm and rain deity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shared Mesoamerican conceptualizations of a flowing, animating sacred energy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Fearsome mother goddesses who embody both vital terrestrial creation and terrifying, unyielding destruction.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A fluid, moving sacred energy that charges all things in the cosmos, operating more like electricity than moral grace.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The earth mother whose body becomes the literal terrestrial landscape humanity walks upon.
GOLD SEAM: Chthonic earth-mothers whose fearsome, unmediated power is expressed through the literal handling and wearing of venomous serpents.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The bearded, pacifistic culture hero who arrives to civilize humanity before mysteriously departing.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The tempestuous deity of the commoners who sends cataclysmic floods to punish human transgressions.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The sovereign solar deity whose cosmic journey depends entirely on the continuous sacrifice of human blood.
GOLD SEAM: The human sovereign functioning strictly as a ritual vessel and conduit for divine resources, bound by exhaustive ceremonial duties.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The terrifying, monstrous mother figure who births major cultural forces but is marked by profound tragic alienation.

Two warrior sons of a self-renewing mother who are born to destroy the enemies threatening her — the Diné Monster Slayer and the Mexica solar warrior, each violence sanctified as protection of the maternal order.
American disorder-bringers and shape-shifters whose meddling drives the world's mutability; Coyote's smaller mischief rhymes with the Smoking Mirror's cosmic disruption, a New-World trickster cast.

The quartered, colour-coded cosmos — four Diné mountains at the cardinal points, four Tezcatlipocas holding the world's corners; the same four-directional cosmogram across separate American traditions.

Both lay the cosmos out as a quartered, four-directional figure — the Diné homeland's four colour-coded peaks and the cross-shaped Patolli board — the same diagram of the world in landscape and in play.

The quieter, water-and-death-shadowed half of a divine pair — Born-for-Water as watcher beside the slayer, Xolotl the underworld twin of Quetzalcoatl; a loose parallel of the second, darker twin, flagged as a reach.
GOLD SEAM: Both require a human vessel to temporarily step aside and become the literal, speaking embodiment of an otherworldly entity.
GOLD SEAM: Indigenous patron deities of warfare who demand captive offerings and define the militant identity of an ascendant empire.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Olmec weeping rain-baby is the direct iconographic and conceptual ancestor of the Aztec and Teotihuacan rain gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A documented iconographic lineage stretching from La Venta directly to the Aztec creator deity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Tezcatlipoca requires the blood of the captive to sustain the cosmos; O9A demands the blood of the 'dross' to feed acausal entities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A terrifying, monstrous mother figure who embodies the absolute, devouring darkness of the cosmos.
Both are dark, shape-shifting trickster-makers entangled with the origin of the lit world; an interpretive reach, the resemblance loose.
An immanent ordering force enacted through ritual rather than a personal deity; loose structural kinship, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both describe a single, dynamic energy that is identical to the cosmos itself, constantly generating reality.

The masked Katsina dancer who BECOMES the spirit and the Mexica ixiptla, the human or image into whom a god is made present, are two American forms of the vessel-of-spirit — the deity bodied forth in a consecrated wearer, independently reached.

A loose parallel: the sacred animating presence the masked dancer takes on rhymes with teotl, the Nahua charged sacred force that fills its vessels — flagged as a reach toward the idea of an immanent power donned, not a documented link.

The Katsinam as cloud-and-rain ancestors who must be danced into presence to bring the water, and Tláloc the Mexica rain-lord of the storied mountaintops, are two Southwestern-Mesoamerican faces of the rain made personal and petitioned by a maize-people.

Skull-faced American lords of death and the earth's lower realm — Maasaw who owns the Fourth World's surface and keeps the dead, Mictlantēcuhtli who rules Mictlan; the death-sovereign of the New World, though Maasaw uniquely also greets and keeps the living.

Bisexual self-existent first beings who contain both sexes and generate the cosmos from their own dual wholeness — the Zuni All-Container 'He-She' and the Nahua Ōmeteōtl 'Two-God'; the androgyne source as one-through-many, separately conceived in two American highlands.

Successive worlds made and destroyed: the Hopi four worlds ruined by corruption and the Mexica suns ended in cataclysm under the four Tezcatlipocas — the American world-ages motif, ascent-by-floors here, recurring-suns there.
GOLD SEAM: Divine recipients of institutionalized child sacrifice, where the state enforces a brutal, mathematical exchange of elite blood for rain, fertility, and cosmic stability.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A skeletal lord of the underworld who consumes the dead and rules over the stark reality of the grave.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions select a human to become the terrifying, living embodiment of a deity for a strictly delimited period of time.

Loose: the lesser, ambivalent god bound up with the dead and with sorcery distantly rhymes with the smoking-mirror god of fate and affliction, but the San figure is far less personified and far less malign. Flagged.

Both are the rain made a being to be ritually fed — but Tlaloc is an enthroned rain-god served with sacrifice, while !Khwa is a rain-animal hunted and slaughtered by a shaman. The same dependence on rain, divinised at opposite poles: god versus quarry.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A commanding mountain deity who directly controls the rains, storms, and the agricultural lifelines of the people.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are disruptive adversaries who use their chaotic influence to challenge complacency and test the moral fiber of humanity.
GOLD SEAM: Both are dark, adversarial forces intricately tied to earthly matters, strife, and the exposing of human hypocrisy.
GOLD SEAM: Fearsome, chthonic earth mothers visually and theologically bound to generative, serpentine imagery.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The terrifying, canine-associated deity of the margins and the grotesque, pointing to transformative underworld powers that defy the neat order of the sky gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dark, unpredictable, norm-shattering lord associated with the night, skulls, and the terrifying destruction of the old order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The terrifying mother adorned with severed human body parts, embodying the brutal, cyclical necessity of death to sustain cosmic life.
GOLD SEAM: Solar deities intimately tied to warfare, state survival, and unconquerable military might, requiring specific rites to sustain their victorious power.
GOLD SEAM: The Summerland radically rejects the concept of a dark, static underworld ruled by a fearsome lord of the dead, replacing it with a bright realm of continuous life.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Conceptualizing the divine not as a static entity, but as a dynamic, vibrating energy that composes and moves all of existence.

The spirit-vessel arrived at twice in the New World: the zemí is the carved idol the spirit inhabits and that is fed and answers, as the ixiptla is the body the teōtl 'puts on' — divinity localised in a material vessel, separately conceived.

One sacred power expressed through many vessels — the zemí as the spirit-in-its-idol rhymes with teōtl, the single divine force whose faces are the gods and whose vessels are the ixiptla; the one-through-many joint reached on both sides of the Caribbean.

Lords of the land of the dead in the same Mesoamerican-Caribbean world — Maquetaurie Guayaba over the night-roaming, guava-eating opía and Mictlāntēcuhtli over the four-year journey of Mictlan; underworld-sovereigns of unjudged, non-fiery realms.

The dog as guide of the dead, reached twice in the New World: Opiyelguabirán the dog-zemi steering the opía to Coaybay and dog-headed Xōlōtl leading the sun and the dead through the Mexica underworld; the canine psychopomp, separately arrived at.

Antillean and Mexica masters of the storm-waters, but inverted in valence: where Tlāloc's rain is the giver of life and a paradise, Guabancex's whirling tempest is catastrophe — the same weather-power split toward fertility there and ruin here.

The principled bringer of disorder within the cosmos — Guabancex's destroying hurricane set against the food-giving order, loosely echoing the Smoking Mirror's cosmic disruption; flagged as a reach across separate traditions.

Culture-heroes whose own affliction makes the world's gifts — scabby Deminán from whose sore back the first ancestress is cut, the Feathered Serpent who burns himself to rise as the morning star; a faint parallel of the suffering culture-bringer, flagged as a reach.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are nocturnal, adversarial figures associated with the manipulation of fate, subjective reality, and testing humanity's individual volition.
A benign creator-maker met at the topmost sky-layer, paired against a destructive double — analogous high-creator function across families.
A single animating sacred force underlying many beings (one-through-many) — analogous impersonal-power concepts across wholly separate worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are nocturnal, adversarial figures who employ temptation, disruption, and forbidden magic to test and strengthen humanity.
GOLD SEAM: Both are fiercely martial, solar deities demanding energetic participation and representing an unapologetic ethos of strength.
GOLD SEAM: Both are symbolized by the Morning Star (Venus) and function as culture heroes who bring awakening and civilization to mankind.
GOLD SEAM: Dark, uncompromising divinities intimately linked to the violent martial reality of the state, demanding human war captives.

A reach across the Americas: both are luminous serpents bound to a shining sky-element rather than to slaying — but Quetzalcoatl is a great creator-civiliser and Mboi-tata a guarding fire-beast; flagged as a loose serpent-of-light rhyme.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A shadowed, often feared entity associated with the margins, tasked with guiding human souls through the dangerous spiritual underworld.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme state patron gods of war whose worship demands immense, formalized tribute to sustain imperial expansion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The eternally youthful, mesmerizing, and dangerously unpredictable supreme lord who playfully manipulates human destinies like a cosmic game.
GOLD SEAM: Skeletal lords of the dead who fuse macabre terror with a profound, leveling cosmic inevitability.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The divine, animating principle that flows from the supreme source, differentiating itself into all temporal and natural phenomena.
GOLD SEAM: Both involve a human actively stepping aside to become the physical, speaking vessel of a deity, rather than just a passive conduit.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The shared Mesoamerican storm and rain deity complex, manifesting with identical goggled eyes and lightning-serpent iconography.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Lord 8 Deer's political legitimation required a literal journey to receive a Toltec nose-piercing, binding Mixtec royalty to the cult of Quetzalcoatl.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The skeletal sovereign of the Mesoamerican underworld, though Lady 9 Grass acts more actively as a political oracle.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dualistic, male-female primordial creator principle of Mesoamerica, existing before all other gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The bundle serves as a literal, physical manifestation or 'impersonator' of the divine force on earth, acting as its proxy.
Both are principled adversaries woven into the cosmos, but Tezcatlipoca is ambivalent fate-and-smoke while Ahriman is unambiguous evil — the contrast sharpens what ethical dualism means.
Both figure origin as an undifferentiated source from which the world is brought forth — but uHlanga is a vegetal reed-matrix, not a dual divinity. A loose world-egg echo, flagged.
Earth/fertility mother tied to the agricultural cycle, but where Coatlicue devours as well as bears, Nomkhubulwane is wholly the green giver — a partial echo, flagged.
The serpent fused with sky and weather; both are world-serpents of cloud and storm rather than mere chaos-dragons, the snake as continuity rather than enemy.

GOLD SEAM: Sahagún (c. 1576) protested that the Guadalupe shrine at Tepeyac continued the cult of Tonantzin, 'Our Mother' — the site-continuity is attested; mapping Tonantzin onto the surviving figure of Coatlicue is approximate.

The popular claim that Santa Muerte descends from Mictecacihuatl and Mictlantecuhtli is CONTESTED — no documented continuity spans the colonial gap (Chesnut 2012); carried here as resonance, not descent.

A reach across the Americas: both are serpents bound to water, rainbows and cosmic renewal rather than to chaos-slaying — but Quetzalcoatl is a creator-civilizer god and Amaru a propitiated natural force; flagged as a loose serpent-of-renewal rhyme.

Child sacrifice as targeted rain- and mountain-propitiation infrastructure in two American empires — the Qhapaq Hucha's frozen mountaintop children and the Aztec Tlaloc cult's child offerings at mountain shrines, independent institutions with a shared logic.
Intra-tradition: husband and wife, the twin faces of Mesoamerican water — his storm and rain, her lakes, rivers and birth-water.
Intra-tradition: co-rulers of Mictlan, husband and wife enthroned together in the lowest underworld.
Intra-tradition: father and son — Mixcoatl fathers Quetzalcoatl by Chimalman in the Anales de Cuauhtitlan.
Intra-tradition: the paired flower deities, female and male faces of beauty, pleasure and the arts.
Intra-tradition: the earthy goddess of transgression-and-confession set against the young, ornamental goddess of pleasure — the two poles of the feminine in Aztec thought.
Intra-tradition: mother and son, the goddess of the earth's filth and the young maize god she bears.
Intra-tradition: companion deities of vegetative renewal presiding over neighboring points of the Aztec agricultural festival calendar.
Intra-tradition: Nanahuatzin's self-immolation at Teotihuacan is the act by which he becomes Tonatiuh — the humble god's death is the sun's birth.
Intra-tradition: Tezcatlipoca, using his own foot as bait, helps seize and tear Tlaltecuhtli in two to forge earth and sky.
Intra-tradition: Quetzalcoatl co-tears Tlaltecuhtli apart with Tezcatlipoca, raising the sky from one half of her body.
Intra-tradition: Tezcatlipoca ruled the First Sun as a sun of jaguars, ended when jaguars devoured its people.
Intra-tradition: Quetzalcoatl ruled the Second Sun, ended by great hurricanes.
Intra-tradition: Tlaloc ruled the Third Sun, ended by a rain of fire.
Intra-tradition: Chalchiuhtlicue ruled the Fourth Sun, ended by flood.
Intra-tradition: Tonatiuh is the Fifth and present Sun, destined to end in earthquake.
Intra-tradition: Nanahuatzin's sacrifice at Teotihuacan is the founding act that inaugurates the Fifth Sun.
Self-wounding renewal-gods of the growing season — Xipe's flayed skin and Attis's self-castration both figure spring fertility as a divine body giving up its old form.
Water-goddesses explicitly titled 'Mother of Waters', presiding over the life-giving and the drowning powers of the same element.
A young grain/staple-crop deity whose growth cycle structures the agricultural festival calendar of an entire civilization.
Enthroned queens of a subterranean land of the dead, ruling not as consorts alone but as sovereigns of a morally neutral underworld.
Fierce mother-goddesses whose martial, death-adjacent power is inseparable from their claim to protect and birth.
Sacred fire treated as an active divine force at the center of both cosmic order and household ritual, requiring perpetual ceremonial maintenance.
World-ages destroyed and remade in succession, catastrophe read as the basic rhythm of cosmic time rather than an aberration.
A cosmos periodically consumed and reconstituted — the Mesoamerican Five Suns and the Stoic conflagration both make the present world one cycle among a recurring series.

A reach: both are monstrous chthonic beings whose dismemberment or feeding founds the world's fertility, but Ai Apaec is a fanged decapitator-creator rather than a torn primal body; the primordial-sacrifice theme only, flagged.
fleshless, black-spotted skeletal death lords of their respective codex traditions, both patrons of the lowest, corpse-bound level of the underworld.
quadripartite, color-coded world-bearing gods at the four directions — the direct Maya counterpart of the Aztec four Tezcatlipocas.
Tonatiuh is the reigning, Fifth chapter of this composite's world-age sequence — the specific deity of the present age the frame describes.
Teotihuacan's Feathered Serpent Pyramid predates and iconographically anchors the later Aztec cult of Quetzalcoatl, whose ruins the Mexica revered as the birthplace of the gods.
Xipe Totec is the Red Tezcatlipoca of the East, one of the four sons who quarter the cosmos in the rostered entry that already names him.
The rebel sister dismembered at birth by the god she and her brothers marched against at Coatepec — the founding defeat behind his solar-warrior cult.
Daughter and mother at the center of the Coatepec myth: Coyolxauhqui leads the attack on pregnant Coatlicue that Huitzilopochtli is born armed to repel.
One of the pair of gods who tear her body in two to make sky and earth.
The other of the pair who tears her body in two — the earth-creation companion myth to the four-Tezcatlipocas' sky-quartering.
The rostered patolli board's 52-space circuit is a game-form of the Calendar Round that the 260-day tonalpohualli anchors.

Some Southwest archaeologists have floated Mesoamerican influence on the Pueblo horned/plumed-serpent motif (the pottery figure sometimes called Awanyu) via down-the-line contact; this remains debated rather than demonstrated, flagged here as a speculative reach.

Both are departed culture-hero/benefactors whose prophesied return carries major political weight; kept at tier 3 since the Quetzalcoatl 'return prophecy' tradition is itself contested by scholars as possibly a post-conquest elaboration, and no contact between the two traditions is claimed.
the shared pan-Mesoamerican Feathered Serpent complex — Lord 9 Wind's flint-birth and temple-founding parallel Quetzalcoatl's own culture-hero and creation roles.
solar war-patrons whose cult underwrites a specific royal dynasty's conquests — Copijcha for Zaachila, Huitzilopochtli for Tenochtitlan. Structural kinship, independent cults.

dossier.essence: The state religion of the Mexica of Tenochtitlan and, more broadly, of Nahuatl-speaking central Mexico in the Late Postclassic (14th to early 16th centuries CE), destroyed as a public cult by the Spanish conquest of 1521. It bound a large pantheon — Huitzilopochtli, Tezcatlipoca, Tlaloc, Quetzalcoatl — to one central claim: the cosmos is fragile and must be fed, human blood and hearts repaying the gods' primordial self-sacrifice to keep the Fifth Sun moving.
dossier.cosmos: Reality has passed through five world-ages ('Suns'), the previous four each destroyed; the present Fifth Sun was born at Teotihuacan from the god Nanahuatzin's self-immolation and is fated to end in earthquakes. The layered cosmos — conventionally thirteen heavens above and nine underworld strata below — is pervaded by teotl, a single dynamic sacred energy of which the gods are concentrated manifestations. Afterlife destination followed manner of death rather than morality: warriors and sacrificial victims joined the sun, the drowned went to Tlaloc's verdant Tlalocan, and most of the dead made a grueling four-year descent to Mictlan ending in dissolution.
dossier.practice: A professional priesthood served temple precincts such as Tenochtitlan's Templo Mayor, coordinating the 365-day solar count and the 260-day divinatory count (tonalpohualli) into a festival round of eighteen monthly feasts. Rites included human sacrifice framed as nextlahualli ('debt-payment'), pervasive autosacrificial bloodletting, deity-impersonation (ixiptla), and warfare partly organized to supply captives. Commoners participated through household ritual, calendrical naming and divination, and the feast cycle.
dossier.canon: No scripture in the textual sense: pre-Columbian pictorial codices (the Codex Borgia group among others) plus post-conquest transcriptions of Nahua oral tradition — above all Sahagun's Florentine Codex (1577), the Leyenda de los Soles (1558), and the Annals of Cuauhtitlan.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds reciprocity pushed to its limit — religion as the literal metabolic maintenance of the cosmos, with humanity cast as permanent debtor.
sources: Bernardino de Sahagun, 'Florentine Codex: General History of the Things of New Spain' (1577)
James Maffie, 'Aztec Philosophy: Understanding a World in Motion' (2014)
David Carrasco, 'City of Sacrifice: The Aztec Empire and the Role of Violence in Civilization' (1999)
